title: How to Measure Falls Prevention ROI: A Formula for Operators
description: Calculate the financial case for falls prevention. Average fall costs $30,000-$50,000. Otago Program returns 144% ROI over 12 months through prevention.

blocks.question: What is the financial case for investing in falls prevention?
blocks.directAnswer: Falls prevention ROI measures the financial return from investing in screening and intervention programs. The average fall costs $30,000 to $50,000 in acute care and liability. Evidence-based multifactorial interventions achieve up to 144% return on investment. Able Assess provides the screening data you need to quantify risk reduction and model financial outcomes for your organization.

tldrBullets.text: Average fall cost: $30,000-$50,000 per incident (hospitalization, litigation, care escalation)
Otago Program: 32% fall reduction, $5,000 average saving per participant annually
Multifactorial interventions (exercise + medication review + home mods): up to 40% reduction, ROI 144%

blocks.expandedAnswer:
Operators often view falls prevention as a cost center, not an investment. This mindset changes when you calculate the true cost of falls. A single serious fall (hip fracture) triggers hospitalization, orthopedic surgery, rehabilitative care, legal liability, and often permanent care escalation. Total cost: $30,000 to $50,000 depending on severity and care setting.

Multiply this by incidence. A 100-resident care home with 30 falls annually at average cost of $40,000 faces $1,200,000 annual falls cost. This includes direct medical costs, litigation insurance, staff time, and family complaints. Prevention investment appears cheap by comparison.

The Otago Exercise Program, the most rigorously studied falls prevention intervention, costs approximately $750-$1,000 per participant annually (equipment, staffing, training). It prevents 32% of falls. For our 100-resident home, if 50 participate and 5-8 falls are prevented annually, savings exceed $190,000. ROI is positive within months.

Abel Care operationalizes this by streamlining assessment, targeting interventions efficiently, and enabling outcome tracking. Organizations measure ROI by comparing pre- and post-intervention fall rates, calculating cost savings, and documenting quality improvements.

sources.title: NHS England: The cost of fractures in the UK
CDC STEADI: Return on Investment for Falls Prevention Programs

blocks.body:
The cost of inaction: what a single fall really costs

A fall that results in hospitalization (hip fracture, head injury, serious laceration) typically costs $30,000 to $50,000. This includes emergency department treatment ($1,000-$1,500), imaging (CT, X-ray, $600-$1,200), surgery ($6,000-$10,000 for orthopedic repair), hospitalization (7-10 days at $400-$500 per night = $2,800-$5,000), rehabilitation (2-4 weeks at $300-$450 per day = $4,200-$12,600), and follow-up outpatient care.

Beyond direct medical costs, a fall triggers indirect costs. Increased staffing (additional care support during recovery), litigation and insurance claims (many facilities face lawsuits following serious falls), potential care escalation (resident moves from independent to residential or nursing care), and family management (complaints, withdrawals, damage to reputation).

Falls prevention costs are trivial by comparison. Otago exercises cost $750-$1,000 per participant annually. Home assessment and modifications cost $600-$2,500 one-time. Medication review costs staff time, not external spend. Assessment tools (paper or digital) are nominal.

Building your ROI model: step by step

Step 1: Calculate baseline fall rate. Count all falls in your setting for the past 12 months. Include falls without injury (many facilities underreport these). For a 100-resident care home, expect 25-35 falls annually if falls prevention is minimal. For 200 community-dwelling clients, expect 40-60 falls. Divide by resident-days to get falls per 1,000 resident-days (a standard metric).

Step 2: Identify fall costs in your context. A community-dwelling older adult who falls and is hospitalized costs the health system $20,000-$30,000. A care home resident costs the home (through insurance, litigation, escalated care) approximately $25,000-$45,000. A home care client costs the home care agency (through liability and staff impact) $12,500-$25,000. Specify your context and costs.

Step 3: Project intervention uptake. If you implement Otago exercises, what percentage of your population will participate? Realistic uptake: 40-60% (some are too unwell, some refuse). For a 100-resident home, assume 50 participants. For 200 community clients, assume 80-100 participants.

Step 4: Apply evidence-based reduction rates. Otago achieves 32% fall reduction in community-dwelling older adults. In residential settings, reduction is typically 20-25% (due to different populations and implementation variability). Multifactorial interventions (Otago + medication review + environmental modifications) achieve 35-40% reduction.

Step 5: Calculate cost savings. Baseline: 30 falls annually in your 100-resident home. Average cost per fall: $40,000. Annual fall cost: $1,200,000. With Otago (50 participants, 25% reduction): 30 × 0.75 = 22.5 falls prevented = 7.5 falls prevented. Savings: 7.5 × $40,000 = $300,000.

Step 6: Subtract intervention costs. Otago delivery: 50 participants × $875 per year = $43,750. Assessment tools: $6,000 (one-time). Training: $4,000. Total first-year cost: $53,750. Subsequent years: $43,750-$47,500.

Step 7: Calculate ROI. Year 1 ROI: ($300,000 savings - $53,750 costs) / $53,750 × 100 = 458% ROI. Even conservative estimates (10 falls prevented instead of 7.5) yield 300% ROI.

Variable inputs: adjust for your setting

Fall rate variability: Care homes for younger residents (40-65) have lower baseline fall rates (10-15 annually per 100 residents) but higher cost per serious fall (longer life expectancy, more litigation). Nursing homes for very frail residents (80+) have higher fall rates (50-80 annually per 100) but lower cost per fall (shorter remaining life, less litigation). Community-dwelling clients are intermediate.

Cost assumptions: Urban areas face higher hospital costs ($30,000-$50,000 per fall). Rural areas may have lower acute costs but higher transport costs. Private insurance liability claims can exceed $65,000. NHS-funded settings experience lower direct costs but reputational damage affects occupancy and revenue.

Uptake constraints: Cognitive impairment reduces Otago uptake and effectiveness. Highly mobile independent residents achieve 40-50% fall reduction. Sedentary frail residents achieve 15-25% reduction. Segment your population by function and apply realistic reduction rates.

Intervention mix: Otago alone achieves 32% reduction. Add medication review (+5-10% additional reduction). Add home modifications (+3-5% additional). Multifactorial approaches compound to 40-45% reduction. Choose interventions matching your population and resources.

Example calculation: a 100-resident care home

Baseline metrics: 30 falls annually (0.3 per resident per year). 15 require hospitalization ($45,000 average each = $675,000). 15 are minor ($10,000 average = $150,000). Total annual cost of falls: $825,000.

Intervention plan: Implement Otago (50 participants, 30 minutes 3× weekly), medication review (all residents), home assessment (all 100). Expected fall reduction: 25% (conservative).

Costs: Otago delivery (physiotherapist 0.8 FTE, equipment): $50,000. Medication review (physician time, 4 hours per resident annually): $20,000. Home assessment (OT 0.3 FTE): $15,000. Assessment tools and training: $6,000. Total year 1: $91,000. Year 2+: $78,000 (no training repeat).

Savings: 30 falls × 0.75 (25% reduction) = 22.5 falls. 11 hospitalization-level falls prevented: 11 × $45,000 = $495,000. 11.5 minor falls prevented: 11.5 × $10,000 = $115,000. Total savings: $610,000.

Year 1 ROI: ($610,000 - $91,000) / $91,000 = 570% ROI. Even accounting for 20% underestimation, ROI exceeds 400%.

Payback period: Investment recovered in less than 2 months. Ongoing savings every year thereafter.

stats.stat: Average hospital cost for a fall-related hip fracture: $30,000-$50,000
Otago Exercise Program reduces falls by 32% in randomized trials
Average savings per Otago participant: $5,000 annually
Multifactorial interventions (exercise + medication + environmental) achieve ROI of 100-144% within 12 months

blocks.body:
Measuring outcomes: tracking your ROI

An ROI model is only valuable if you measure actual outcomes. Implement a falls tracking system: date, time, location, mechanism, injuries, outcome (no injury, minor, hospitalization), and cost. Digital systems enable rapid analysis.

Compare year-on-year fall rates (falls per 1,000 resident-days). A 25-30% reduction is realistic and meaningful. Calculate actual cost savings: prevented hospitalizations, reduced litigation claims, lower staff absence (falls increase staff injury and stress).

Document secondary benefits: resident confidence improves (measured by activity levels), family satisfaction improves (fewer complaints), staff turnover improves (lower stress), and occupancy stability improves (fewer escalations or transfers out). These intangible benefits often exceed direct cost savings.

Use these data to refine your model. Year 2 projections can be more accurate than Year 1 estimates. Share results with leadership, staff, and families. ROI data justify continued investment and help recruit staff to prevention programs.

items.question: How do we account for staff injuries during falls?
items.answer: Staff injuries are significant hidden costs. Estimate 10-20% of falls injure staff (back strain, lacerations, fractures). Staff injury costs: absence cover, workers compensation, retraining. Add $2,500-$6,000 per staff injury to your fall cost estimate. This strengthens the ROI case.

items.question: What if our fall rate doesn't improve despite interventions?
items.answer: Reassess implementation. Otago requires consistent delivery: 3 times weekly for 30 minutes. If attendance is sporadic, benefits diminish. Poor medication review (medications not actually deprescribed) yields no benefit. Environmental recommendations ignored. Review fidelity before concluding interventions don't work.

items.question: How do we value litigation risk reduction?
items.answer: Litigation costs are hard to quantify but significant. A serious fall lawsuit costs $40,000-$125,000 in legal fees, settlements, and insurance premiums. Prevention reduces litigation exposure. Conservative estimate: each prevented serious fall eliminates $6,000-$20,000 litigation risk. Include this in your savings calculation.

items.question: Should we calculate ROI per resident or per intervention?
items.answer: Calculate both. Organization-level ROI shows financial benefit. Per-resident ROI (cost per fall prevented per resident) helps prioritize interventions. Otago might have $1,750 per resident cost and prevent 0.4 falls per participant, yielding $4,375 cost per fall prevented. Cost-effective by all measures.

items.question: How long before we see ROI positive results?
items.answer: Most organizations see positive ROI within 4-6 months if fall reduction is 20%+ and baseline fall costs are high. In low-fall-rate settings, ROI takes longer. However, intangible benefits (improved resident satisfaction, staff morale) appear immediately.

blocks.heading: Track falls prevention ROI systematically
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess guides your assessment and intervention processes while tracking outcomes, so you can measure ROI with real data from your population.
